event: Polynesian voyagers, likely from Samoa, Tonga, and the Cook Islands, settle the three atolls of Atafu, Nukunonu, and Fakaofo.
Distinct chiefly societies develop on each atoll, with Fakaofo emerging as a dominant power exerting influence over Atafu and Nukunonu.
A god named Tui Tokelau is venerated across the atolls, with a large coral slab idol erected at Fakaofo as the focus of religious life.
British Commodore John Byron aboard HMS Dolphin sights Atafu, naming it the Duke of York's Island, the first recorded European contact.
Captain Edward Edwards of HMS Pandora, searching for the Bounty mutineers, visits Atafu and sights Nukunonu, naming it the Duke of Clarence's Island.
British naval officer Captain Smith aboard HMS Vincennes visits Nukunonu and records details of the atoll.
American whaling captain Smith of the General Jackson sights Fakaofo, which he names D'Wolf's Island.
The United States Exploring Expedition under Charles Wilkes surveys the Tokelau atolls and documents the inhabitants and their society.
Whaling ships increasingly visit the atolls, introducing trade goods, new diseases, and disruptions to traditional island life.
Catholic missionaries from the Samoa-based mission begin efforts that lead to the eventual conversion of Nukunonu to Roman Catholicism.
Protestant missionaries from the London Missionary Society, working through Samoan teachers, establish Christianity on Atafu and Fakaofo.
Peruvian blackbirding slave raiders abduct nearly all able-bodied men from the atolls, devastating the population and ending traditional governance.
Surviving and immigrant Samoans and Europeans help repopulate the atolls, and Christianity becomes firmly established across Tokelau.
Britain claims a vague jurisdiction over Tokelau under the Western Pacific High Commission, though without effective administration.
Captain Oldham of HMS Egeria formally declares a British protectorate over the Tokelau atolls, then known as the Union Islands.
Tokelau, then called the Union Islands, is annexed and incorporated into the British colony of the Gilbert and Ellice Islands.
Administrative responsibility for Tokelau is transferred from the Gilbert and Ellice Islands to New Zealand by an Order in Council.
New Zealand formally takes over administration of the three atolls, governing them through the colonial administration of Western Samoa.
The Tokelau Islands Act formally incorporates the atolls into the territorial boundaries of New Zealand, granting residents New Zealand citizenship.
Tokelauans become New Zealand citizens, establishing a permanent constitutional link with New Zealand that continues to the present day.
Swains Island, geographically and culturally part of Tokelau, remains under United States administration as part of American Samoa, dividing the archipelago.
Administrative responsibility for Tokelau is transferred from New Zealand's Maori and Island Affairs department to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.
The territory's official name is changed from the Tokelau Islands to simply Tokelau.
The Treaty of Tokehega between New Zealand and the United States resolves competing claims, with the US relinquishing claims to the three atolls.
The Treaty of Tokehega is signed, formalizing the maritime boundary and confirming Swains Island as part of American Samoa.
Tokelau establishes the General Fono as a national representative body drawing delegates from all three atoll councils.
Tokelau is identified by scientists as among the world's nations most vulnerable to sea-level rise, given its maximum elevation of about five meters.
New Zealand begins devolving administrative powers to Tokelau's local institutions, strengthening the role of the General Fono.
The Tokelau Amendment Act grants the General Fono the power to make rules for the peace, order, and good government of Tokelau.
Further legislative powers are devolved to the General Fono, advancing Tokelau's progress toward greater self-government.
Tokelau and New Zealand agree to explore a free association arrangement, similar to those of Niue and the Cook Islands.
Tokelau and New Zealand begin drafting a constitution and a treaty of free association in preparation for a self-determination referendum.
Cyclone Percy strikes Tokelau, causing severe flooding and damage across the low-lying atolls and underscoring climate vulnerability.
A UN-supervised self-determination referendum on free association with New Zealand falls just short of the required two-thirds majority.
A second self-determination referendum again narrowly fails to reach the two-thirds threshold, leaving Tokelau a New Zealand territory.
The role of Ulu-o-Tokelau, the titular head of government, begins rotating annually among the three faipule (atoll leaders).
Tokelau declares a state of emergency as a severe drought leaves the atolls nearly out of fresh water, prompting emergency shipments.
Tokelau commissions solar power installations on all three atolls, aiming to become one of the world's first fully solar-powered nations.
Tokelau continues to be listed by the United Nations as one of the remaining Non-Self-Governing Territories awaiting decolonization.
Tokelau imposes strict border controls and remains free of community transmission of COVID-19 throughout the global pandemic.
Tokelau intensifies climate adaptation efforts, including seawall construction and coastal protection, against accelerating sea-level rise.
Tokelau and New Zealand reaffirm their constitutional relationship and ongoing cooperation on climate resilience and development.
